issue_title: `toml.loads` spins forever parsing a table entry with whitespace and dots
issue_body:
On 0.9.4, adding a table entry value with (unnecessary) padding whitespace and dots (which looks like the spec allows?) seems to end up in an infinite loop:

```
Python 3.6.4 (default, Dec 19 2017, 14:48:12) 
[GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-16)] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import toml
>>> toml.__version__
'0.9.4'
>>> toml.loads('[ "white.space" ]')
```

At that point, it spins forever (or until I ctrl-c it).

It does just seem to be the combination of the two; either on their own is fine:

```
>>> toml.loads('["no.white.space"]')
{'no.white.space': {}}
>>> toml.loads('[ "nodot" ]')
{'nodot': {}}
```

issue_title: furl do not handle multiple parameters of the same name

If I use and URL like the following:

    url = 'http://localhost:8080/foo?Subject=aa&Subject=bb'
    f = furl(url)

...I have no way to manipulate the query string values.

calling `f.args.Subject` is only returning the first item.

hi,
I noticed that ```dccreceive.py``` sends some weird characters in [line 48](https://github.com/jaraco/irc/blob/master/scripts/dccreceive.py#L48) whenever it receives a chunk of data. I couldn't find anything hinting at that in the DCC specifications and, in my tests, this actually disturbed the file transmission. What's the reason for sending this?

issue_title: Tests are continually run, not continuously
issue_body:
Your README has this:

>Tests are continuously run using Travis-CI.

There's a slight difference between "continuously" and "continually", and y'all are using the wrong one. Basically, continuously means "no gaps" where "continually" means "regularly, but often". So, road construction would be continual, because the workers go home at night. A mathematical function might be continuous if it doesn't have gaps.

If tests were continuously run, that would mean that the testing process never terminates (and thus would never give a result). This would defeat the point of testing. I'm guessing this isn't what you mean. Continual testing would mean that tests are run periodically and regularly (probably whenever you push a commit).

http://www.vocabulary.com/articles/chooseyourwords/continual-continuous/

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/issue/51
- Originally reported by: Peter Harpending
- Originally created at: 2015-01-14T14:31:48.415

issue_title: How to get the status of a unit or a unit change?
issue_body:
My goal is to write a small script that starts and stops a unit, checks if the job was successful and periodically checks the status of the unit. I hope that you could assist me in that. I would add the finished code to the examples, if you like, to give something back to the project.

This is what I have so far.
```python
from pydbus import SystemBus
bus = SystemBus()
systemd = bus.get(".systemd1")
job1 = systemd.StopUnit("ssh.service", "fail")
job2 = systemd.StartUnit("ssh.service", "fail")
```

The issues that remain and where I need some help are:

1. How do I check if `StartUnit()` was successful? I see that I get a job number and I assume that somehow I will get that information using the job?
2. How do I check the status of a unit? Basically the information that `systemctl status ssh.service` prints to the terminal.

I tried to define units programmatically and encountered problems.
For now we can construct a string and pass it to ```define```. It discards the info about types like ```Decimal```, is ugly, potentially can cause troubles.

The right way to do it is to have API.

1 the API to define a base unit
```python
def defineBaseUnit(self, unitName, quantityName)
```
2 the API to define any unit
```python
def __setattr__(self, unitName, unitValue)
```
The look&feel
```python
ureg.USD=ureg.RUR*Decimal(65)
```

issue_title: Not seeing the "did you mean" predictions.
issue_body: When I miss type I don't see the "did you mean" message showing me the possible fixes. When I type `fuck` however it still works. System Details: - macOS: 10.12.4 (Beta) - bash shell Is there something I'm missing?

Say I was using FoBiS to compile libraries which are in turn used by multiple programs (an example can be found [here](https://github.com/jacobwilliams/Fortran-Astrodynamics-Toolkit/blob/master/build.sh)).  I believe currently, if I make a change to a module within the library, the main programs are not recompiled (since they are only linking to the ```.a``` library file that is produced by a previous FoBiS call, and have no knowledge of how they are interdependent).  Is there currently any way to tell FoBiS that one project depends on another?  (Note: I'm getting this idea from [foraytool](https://code.google.com/p/foraytool/) which has this feature).

psychopy has built-in timers/clocks, but I wonder if it would be nice to have a thing accounting for the discretization of visual events and dropped frames.

I think we could just count frames for event timing, e.g.
`after 10 frames, draw the circle`
but that's not super intuitive, and subject to differences in frame rate.

If you were to do by clock/timer, e.g.
`after 160 ms, draw the circle`
then I think the drawing call can end up too close to the `win.flip()`, which results in the image landing on the subsequent frame.

I think a desirable API would take the desired stimulus time, auto-interpret as frame count (accounting for different refresh rates), and log/warn if the requested time is not exactly on the proper interval, e.g. drawing at 14ms rather than 16.666...ms.

Pseudocode follows:
```python
# 9.6 frames, round up to 10 and warn
frame_stimulus = 60 f/s * 0.16 s

# take ceiling (be consistent!) & cast to int (not necessary?)
frame_stimulus = int(np.ceil(frame_stimulus)) 

# if the next frame is the correct one, be ready to draw
if frame + 1 >= frame_stimulus, draw the circle
```
Good reading: http://lindeloev.net/psychopy-course/stimulus-precision/timing-in-experiments/

<also discuss timing relative to other events, link to audio-visual synchronization, ...>

Callables are not currently working, as referenced in #3.

Thinking about callable objects, I think the best way to handle them is as follows.

Let's say we have some function that takes another function as an argument with correct type hinting syntax.

```python
def foo(func: Callable[[int, int], float], s: str) -> str:
    return s + str(func(5, 5))
```

I think that the best way to handle this is to also require that the argument passed in `func` needs to also have complete type annotation.

```python
def func(x: int, y: int) -> float:
    return float(x * y)
```

With `func` possibly also checked at runtime depending on whether or not the `enforce.runtime_validation` decorator is applied. The only thing that the high level function `foo` checks is that the call signature of `func` matches the type hinting specified.

How's that sound?

@heitzmann As we go between gdspy versions and our own software versions, we validate each transition by regenerating all .gds file masks and comparing if their content has changed compared to when they were originally created. This allows us to detect any software/packages bugs. In order to achieve this, .gds files are hashed using SHA-1 and then the hashes are compared. However, we have encountered the following challenge: .gds files appear to be always different even if they are generated by the same code. The difference was traced to the timestamp that is embedded by gdspy when .gds files are written. Our attempts to exclude timestamps within the binary files, and compare the rest of binary content, runs into difficulties because of structural differences of various files. Could you please advise on how best to tackle this issue?

issue_title: Negative timer values due to time.time
issue_body:
We're seeing very occasional negative timer values due to NTP shifts and calls of time.time to gettimeofday(), rough background on that [here](https://blog.habets.se/2010/09/gettimeofday-should-never-be-used-to-measure-time.html).

[perf_counter](https://docs.python.org/3/library/time.html#time.perf_counter) provides the highest resolution wall time available in Python 3, fixing this for 2 seems a little harder.  [clock](https://docs.python.org/2/library/time.html#time.clock) seems to be the logical choice, it is also based off wall time, however it looks as though it suffers resolution problems according to the timeit docs [here](https://docs.python.org/2/library/timeit.html#timeit.default_timer).  Without wanting a resolution regression this bug/edgecase will likely have to stay.

Comparing the performance between time.time and time.perf_counter doesn't show any performance regression.

```
In [57]: timeit.timeit('perf_counter()', setup='from time import perf_counter', number=10000000)
Out[57]: 2.137723121792078

In [58]: timeit.timeit('time()', setup='from time import time', number=10000000)
Out[58]: 2.1814921628683805
```

I'll open a PR with a fix, I'm not sure how I'd write a test for this however.

issue_title: strace-based netlink debug method doesn't work with strace >= 4.13
issue_body:
Hello,
I'm running Debian Testing with strace 4.15
I tried to follow the instructions at [http://docs.pyroute2.org/debug.html](http://docs.pyroute2.org/debug.html).

It appears that the strace developers added some logic to decode netlink messages in [commit 2850f745](https://github.com/strace/strace/commit/2850f745ca49f52b4b1266a6a1b1ec0c58f77c66), which corresponds to version 4.13.
This is neat in itself, but it causes strace's output to leave out the message header, as detailed below.

Here's an excerpt from running `strace -e trace=network -x -s 16384 ip route show`:
`recvmsg(3, {msg_name={sa_family=AF_NETLINK, nl_pid=0, nl_groups=00000000}, msg_namelen=12, msg_iov=[{iov_base=[{{len=52, type=0x18 /* NLMSG_??? */, flags=NLM_F_MULTI, seq=1488792959, pid=2658}, "\x02\x00\x00\x00\xfe\x03\x00\x01\x00\x00\x00\x00\x08\x00\x0f\x00\xfe\x00\x00\x00\x08\x00\x05\x00\x0a\x04\x01\x01\x08\x00\x04\x00\x06\x00\x00\x00"},`
As you can see, it prints some information such as the message length, type and flags but strips the message header in the hex dump (the length is supposed to be 52 but the printed hex output is only 36 bytes long). This behavior appears to be hardcoded.

As a result, copying and pasting strace's output in files and running decoder.py on them doesn't work anymore.

Could you mention it in the documentation ? I can open a pull request for this if you want.
Even better, do you have a workaround (other than using an older strace version, of course) ? I was thinking about something based on nlmon but I just stumbled upon this today and haven't had time yet to think this through.

issue_title: [XServer] Library makes program hard crash when monitor is out of bounds
issue_body:
General information:

* OS name: _Ubuntu (`ubuntu-desktop`)_
* OS version: __18.04 LTS__
* OS architecture: _64 bits_
* Resolutions:
    * Monitor 1: _1920x1080 (laptop built-in)_
    * Monitor 2: _1366x768 (external)_
* Python version: _3.6.5_
* MSS version: __3.2.0__


For GNU/Linux users:

* Display server protocol and version, if known: __X server__ _(X.Org version: 1.19.6)_
* Desktop Environment: _Gnome 3_
* Composite Window Manager name and version: __???__ (The default one, I guess?)


### Description of the warning/error
This error occurs when the a monitor that is out of bounds is specified in the `grab()` call.
```python
from mss import mss

screenshotter = mss()
monitor = {
    "left": -30,
    "top": 0,
    "width": 100,
    "height": 100,
}
try:
    screenshotter.grab(monitor)  # X Error
except BaseException as e:
    # Never reached
    print("Handling error")
```
Using a monitor like `{"left": 0, "top": 0, "width": 10000, "height": 100}` has the same result.
### Full message
```stderr
X Error of failed request:  BadMatch (invalid parameter attributes)
  Major opcode of failed request:  73 (X_GetImage)
  Serial number of failed request:  91
  Current serial number in output stream:  91
```
The message is printed to `stderr`, then the Python interpreter quits with exit code `1`.

### Other details
I discovered this because I let my program generate a box based on the location of the cursor. Then I take the screenshot of only the cursor area.

While it might be better for efficiency not to check the bounds of the monitor, I personally don't think causing this type of hard crash is acceptable. It's up to you. If you decide not to check bounds, I think a mention of this in the documentation would be appropriate.

I'm not a pro by any means, but I think for most projects on GitHub the standard MO is to a) have all releases be tagged appropriately and b) have the active branch (if it is not master) be the default branch.

Is there any reason it's not like that for blessings too?

issue_title: Logging DB warnings to Python warnings module causes memory leak
issue_body:
The cursors module logs warnings from the database to the Python `warnings` system (https://github.com/farcepest/MySQLdb1/blob/master/MySQLdb/cursors.py#L117). The warnings module has the "feature" that it keeps a registry of all encountered warnings (https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/2.7/Lib/warnings.py#l233). This means that if the database produces warnings with dynamic content, the `__warningregistry__` will leak memory.

Since the DB usually does not log that many warnings (if used properly), this used to not be a big deal. But with MySQL 5.6.27 there was a significant increase in warnings. I suspect this is connected to the following entry in the MySQL release notes:

>  MySQL sometimes produced no warning when it was unable to interpret a character in a given character set. (Bug #20238729)

This means that if you e.g. do a query like this (uuid is type ` binary(16)`):
```
cnx.query('select * from FOOBAR where uuid=%s limit 1', '\x00\xbe\x17\xae\x80\xb2F\xbe\xaa\xb0E\xfb\xe9\xae:\x95')
```
you will get a warning like this:
```
Warning(u"Invalid utf8mb4 character string: 'BE17AE'",)
```
which will be stored in `__warningregistry__` forever.

One solution to this problem could be to make the warning mechanism configurable (defaulting to the current behaviour)

issue_title: clockwise path orientation is inverted when doing union
issue_body:
Hello,

I noticed that if a polygon has clockwise orientation and is "black" (not a hole nested inside another polygon), after it is sent through a union operation, it returns with the orientation flipped to counter-clockwise.

```python
import pyclipper

pc = pyclipper.Pyclipper()
# polygon has clockwise orientation
pc.AddPath(((194, 510), (322, 0), (66, 0)), pyclipper.PT_SUBJECT)
solution = pc.Execute(pyclipper.CT_UNION)

print(solution)
# returned polygon has counter-clockwise orientation
# [[[194, 510], [66, 0], [322, 0]]]

# same result here
print(pyclipper.SimplifyPolygon(((194, 510), (322, 0), (66, 0))))
```

The example above only has one subject path so does nothing, but it's enough to show the problem.
It looks like clipper "normalizes" the orientation to counter-clockwise for such polygons.
Is this the way it's supposed to be?

Most users probably won't care since it doesn't change the appearance of the resulting polygon (it's filled in both cases), but in the context of font editing software where some glyphs (letterforms) can be reused as "components" or references inside other glyphs, then the path orientation matters. Because, when two components overlap with each other, it's the path orientation of the "base" glyphs that determines the way such "composite" glyphs will be rendered (e.g. a single black shape, or with cuts and holes).

issue_title: Analytics idea: alter all values at prediction time by one std up and down
issue_body: It let's us get at the linear model question of "holding all else equal, an increase in this value of XYZ translates to an expected difference in our output variable of ABC"

right now, if we are asked to make a prediction for a category we were not trained on, it fails. 

that's somewhat reasonable, and behavior we could just tell the user they have to fix (if getting a prediction for a category the model was not trained on, you are responsible for either not asking for that prediction, or setting the category to something that was trained on that's the closest fallback). 

but, we can probably make the user's life easier. 

take in a `default_category`. this category will be the category we use to get predictions for a row if the category in that row wasn't part of our training dataset. Think of this like geographical expansion. If you expand into a new state that wasn't part of your training set, you can either manually set the state for those rows to be the closest reasonable thing, or, if you want to be a bit lazier, we can just by default fall back on one of the predictors we've already trained. 

make one of the values we can pass in "ERROR", which will raise an error when being passed a row where we do not recognize the category. 

v2 of this feature is to automatically pick the default for the user. This should probably just be the largest category. v3 can have a fancier algo for picking the default category.

issue_title: request param validation should accept lists of tuples
issue_body:
Thanks for the lovely library!

I found a limitation that should be simple to fix.
Many DHIS2 API endpoints allow repeated parameters, but this library does not.

For example, table `1.29` in the `1.17.6. Reading data values` section of [DHIS2 API docs](https://docs.dhis2.org/2.26/en/developer/html/dhis2_developer_manual_full.html) describes several parameters that "Can be repeated any number of times." (such as `dataSet`, `orgUnit`, `period`, etc)

Luckily, the `requests` library supports repeated parameters if the caller provides as a list of tuples: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/user/quickstart/#more-complicated-post-requests

However, the [_validate_request](https://github.com/davidhuser/dhis2.py/blob/master/dhis2/api.py#L169) method raises if `params` is not a `dict`

TypeError: must be str, not bytes
```

To fix this, you need to decode child's output at `io._ContinuousReader._capture_output():82`:

```
                if self._file_out is not None:

```

issue_title: Handling more than one output, but only one loss
issue_body:
I have a model that has three outputs (named "usage", "clients", and "sessions") and one loss function (mean absolute percentage error.) As shown in the picture below, the names of my plots are a little messed up. I believe the upper right plot should be titled "usage_loss".
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19804455/38181849-5c875b0c-3603-11e8-9d44-dbabc9719ea0.png)

I think the problem is in this section of the code, but I'm not sure how to fix it.

https://github.com/stared/livelossplot/blob/fd72c53bd82d0c0052478c735edd356892f6f7f6/livelossplot/keras_plot.py#L54-L59

(After #176 and some solution to #178, this is the last thing preventing ZeroDeploy from at least successfully constructing a remote connection to a Mac; I don't know if it's sufficient to make everything actually work…)

`stat -c` is a GNU extension.

In fact, `stat` itself is a NetBSD/FreeBSD extension that GNU copied with different parameters, but I'll get to that later.

On *BSD, you use `-f` to specify a format string. And instead of having a bunch of variations of each field, you have a smaller set of fields and a bunch of modifiers that can be applied to any of them—e.g., instead of `%u` for uid and `%U` for username, there's `%u` for user and `%Su` for string user. Also, the values for BSD `%HT` aren't exactly the same as the ones for GNU `%F`, but fortunately the three that you check for are the same. So, to make the three `stat`-based methods in `BaseRemoteMachine` to work, you just need to change the arguments to (respectively):

 * `-f'%u,%Su'`
 * `-f'%g,%Sg'`
 * `'%HT,%Xp,%i,%d,%l,%u,%g,%z,%a,%m,%c'`

Meanwhile, for other platforms like Solaris, there is no `stat` command at all. I suppose you could write something that parses the output of `ls -l`, which I think should be sufficient for the places you're actually using it in `plumbum` and `rpyc`—but not for code that users have written that depend on a real `stat` result.

Also, I'm not sure how to patch this. The try/fallback idea I suggested for `env -0` in #178 doesn't seem like a good idea for every single `stat` call. So you probably want to do something like this:

    if self.uname.startswith('Linux') or self.uname.startswith('CYGWIN'):
        # do GNU stuff
    elif self.uname == 'Darwin' or 'BSD' in self.uname:
        # do BSD stuff
    else:
        # do fallback stuff

But doing this same set of checks in at least four places (these three methods, plus the `env` one) doesn't seem like a good idea. Maybe we want to centralize the tests in `self.gnu_userland()` and `self.bsd_userland()` functions? (Of course these two userlands aren't monolithically consistent, but if you stick to things that were in, respectively, glibc 2.1 and both NetBSD 2.0 and FreeBSD 5.0, that's probably safe enough, I hope.)

issue_title: Statistical distribution tests have a massive case of multiple testing
issue_body:
This isn't a correctness problem because it increases the set of false positive failures. It's annoying because it makes the build flaky though!

Proposed plan of action:

   * Make individual tests run at a much stricter significance level. e.g. divide it by the number of tests.
   * Use the Benjamini–Hochberg–Yekutieli procedure to do a statistical test on the p values for all the tests to see if any of them are significant with a 1% false positive rate.

## Issue
Even with the best efforts to get around the possible race condition in `_refresh_visitor`, there still seems to be a chance for an error on `Visitor.save()`. In the following traceback, you can see that after the `IntegrityError` is caught, when `Visitor.objects.get(pk=session_key)` is called,  the Visitor with that key no longer exists. This means either a) the `IntegrityError` is being raised for another reason other than the assumed clashing session keys, which I can't see how that could happen, or b) There's some weird race condition in which `.save()` raises an `IntegrityError` but when we try to get the Visitor it doesn't exist yet. 

## Stacktrace
```
DoesNotExist: Visitor matching query does not exist.
  File "django/core/handlers/base.py", line 223, in get_response
    response = middleware_method(request, response)
  File "newrelic/hooks/framework_django.py", line 331, in wrapper
    return wrapped(*args, **kwargs)
  File "tracking/middleware.py", line 141, in process_response
    visitor = self._refresh_visitor(user, request, now)
  File "tracking/middleware.py", line 98, in _refresh_visitor
    visitor = Visitor.objects.get(pk=session_key)
  File "django/db/models/manager.py", line 127, in manager_method
    return getattr(self.get_queryset(), name)(*args, **kwargs)
  File "django/db/models/query.py", line 334, in get
    self.model._meta.object_name
```

## Example Context at time of error

* *ip_address*:
None
* *request*: 
WSGIRequest at 0x139752192184104
* *self*:
tracking.middleware.VisitorTrackingMiddleware object at 0x7f1a9e789cc0

'3ylrw1m7oad6fxs71mcenhxfsosnyx8o'

-0.004183
* *user*:
None

'Mozilla/5.0 (compatible; bingbot/2.0; +http://www.bing.com/bingbot.htm)'
* *visit_time*:	
datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 30, 23, 13, 22, 232516, tzinfo=<UTC>)
* *visitor*:
Visitor: Visitor object

## Related Code Blocks
middleware.py, lines 140-141
```
# update/create the visitor object for this request
        visitor = self._refresh_visitor(user, request, now)
```
middleware.py, lines 90-98
```
try:
    visitor.save()
except IntegrityError:
    # there is a small chance a second response has saved this
    # Visitor already and a second save() at the same time (having
    # failed to UPDATE anything) will attempt to INSERT the same
    # session key (pk) again causing an IntegrityError
    # If this happens we'll just grab the "winner" and use that!
    visitor = Visitor.objects.get(pk=session_key)
```

## Note
This only occurs when search bots are indexing our site. I'm pretty sure that the rapid request/response cycle combined with a race condition is causing this error.

issue_title: Idea for a sorting utility for IA items
issue_body:
A script that takes a search query, and a format-string of fields to display for each item, and up to four functions mapped to arrow keys, that dispose of the item in different ways.

For spam sorting, left could be "not-spam" (i.e. safelist), right could be "spam" (either explicitly darking it if the user has permissions, or creating a sensibly formatted email if not), and up would be "unclear", which could be reviewed with a more detailed format-string later.

The email-generator could take the search query and format string and make a sensible header out of them, followed by a newline separated list of identifiers (or maybe something else if wanted).

The outputs could be files, with names generated from the timestamp (in IA format, natch) and the search query, and the name of the output function.

The script would extract the mentioned fields from the format string and create the search query appropriately.

issue_title: ia metadata --spreadsheet= should clear metadata fields which have become empty
issue_body:
This is a follow-on from #179 and is a response to the last comment regarding `ia metadata`

As I understand it `ia metadata --spreadsheet=` will not currently clear individual components of previously uploaded multi-part metadata. At least, this is my interpretation of what happened when I tried to use it to correct a problem caused by issue #179.

The scenario I face is this:
- an item is uploaded with e.g. three `subject` fields (which come from `tags` in our episode database)
- for some reason one of those fields is erroneous and we wish to remove it
- we regenerate the metadata which now has two `subject` fields, while the uploaded version has three
- applying the updated metadata should modify the list of subjects to omit the one deleted in the CSV data
- this does not happen

This behaviour has been particularly relevant recently given that issue #179 was allowing fields to "leak" from one row in the CSV file to another.

Applying these subjects to these items using `ia metadata`:
```
hpr2308: guix,linux,nix,sysadmin,development
hpr2309: Accessibility, scripting, audio editing, speech-to-text
hpr2310: Kdenlive,opensource.com,workflow
```
Still leaves them with the extra "leakage":
```
hpr2309: Accessibility, scripting, audio editing, speech-to-text, development
hpr2310: Kdenlive,opensource.com,workflow, speech-to-text, development
```

Having used `ias3upload.pl` for several years prior to the (welcome!) arrival of `ia` I know that when presented with the same changes as described above, this script will remove the "leaked" subjects.

I hope this helps to clarify why such behaviour is desirable in `ia`.

As discussed in #11 and pointed out by @frankherfert , plots generated by scikit-plot are quite small on Jupyter notebook. Adding a `figsize` parameter will let the user adjust the size of the plot based on his/her preferences. 

Generally, `figsize` could be kept at a default value of 6. Make sure to adjust font size of all text elements as well (title, xlabel, ylabel, etc.) to the same value as `figsize`.

issue_title: More detailed log when poll function fails
issue_body:
When PollExecutor is used and the poll function fails, it'll currently log only the message of the caught exception, like this:

``
DEBUG:PollExecutor:Poll function failed: <the message>
``

I think this should be updated to include backtrace as well, otherwise it's hard to track down the reason for a failure.

issue_title: Cross-Compatibility of Python 2 and 3 in a single folder.
issue_body:
Hi, I have a corporate project that requires maintain of compatibility between 2.7 and 3.X (Mainly 3.6.Y).

Due to Firewall policy, I could not use pip install X for installing various dependencies that requires futures. This includes _apscheduler_ etc.

The only way for me to install these modules is via `--install-lib` (More on:
 [https://docs.python.org/2/install/index.html](url)) option with the use of environment variable `PythonPath` with the use of .tar / .zip version.


So, I have installed both Python 2.7 and 3.6 on separate folders in _C:\_ and a common Module Path at _C:\Python_Modules_. This will result _apscheduler_ crash for Python 3 version. This is because _apscheduler_ for Python 3 tries to import futures from Python 2 egg regardless if I tried to compile Futures for Python 3 or not.

Attempted to compile Futures in Python 3 resulted a redundancy warning.
![redundant for python3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25880359/32875780-a851b90a-cad4-11e7-8fdc-b0046c3cd970.png)

The first one is:
`raise exception_type, self._exception, self._traceback` in line 381 of _base.py
![first issue](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25880359/32875527-67d940e2-cad3-11e7-961c-4a9a387992c5.png)

The fix is to change the file (futures-3.1.1\build\lib\concurrent\futures\_base.py) with these:
1. Change the line 381 code to:
`raise_with_traceback(exception_type(self._exception))`
2: Add this line of code below line 9 (`import types`) so as to import the traceback feature.
`from future.utils import raise_with_traceback`

The changed code (Change the extension from txt to py) can be downloaded here:
[_base.txt](https://github.com/agronholm/pythonfutures/files/1477434/_base.txt)



The second one is the issue of the Queue and queue for Python 2 and 3 compatibility.
![second issue](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25880359/32876004-afbc334a-cad5-11e7-9099-ea291f8277a3.png)

This is also faced by other modules such as [https://github.com/docker/compose/issues/2108](url)

The fix is to change the file (futures-3.1.1\build\lib\concurrent\futures\thread.py) with this on line 8:
```
try:
	import Queue as queue
except ImportError:
	import queue
```

The changed code (Change the extension from txt to py) can be downloaded here:
[thread.txt](https://github.com/agronholm/pythonfutures/files/1477458/thread.txt)


With these changes on both files, I will able to solve this issue of futures module not able to use in Python 3.

issue_body: Currently, it's just a `TextField` which means that in some code paths we'll only return the  first 256 bytes, but this field can contain much more. Change it to a field that has `is_complex=True`.

In cases where the `pk` field of a given model is not an int, the `object_id_int` is not being used. If a lot of rows exists for this model, it makes the lookups very very slow.

These look ups can be made considerably faster by adding a `db_index=True` attribute to the text field.

It seems `South` does allow creating a schemamigration for this and it runs with sqlite and postgresql. Also, [Django adds a type specific index](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/databases/#indexes-for-varchar-and-text-columns) (if db supports it).

Is there any reason why this can't be added?

issue_title: 0 converted to empty string during URL construction
issue_body:
First of all, django-js-reverse is amazing! The JS files now look more readable because of it.

However, recently, a strange issue came up while passing 0 (zero) as an argument to the URL -- it got translated to an empty string. In particular, I have this URL pattern:
```python
url(r'^plot/hop_cnt_seq_num/(?P<src_node>\d+)/(?P<src_port>\d+)/(?P<dst_node>\d+)/(?P<dst_port>\d+)/$', 'hop_count_pkt_seq_num', name='trace_hop_cnt',)
```

When I invoke 
```js
Urls.trace_hop_cnt(src_node, src_port, dst_node, dst_port)
```
from within JS with the tuple (66, 0, 67, 0), the resulting URL becomes `/plot/hop_cnt_seq_num/66//67//`, which actually should be `/plot/hop_cnt_seq_num/66/0/67/0/`. However, when invoked with (66, 1, 67, 1), it generates the correct URL `/plot/hop_cnt_seq_num/66/1/67/1/`. So somehow zeroes are being treated otherwise.

Is there any work-around for this? All other URLs are handled fine. I can manually generate the URL (like I was doing previously) as
```js
['/plot/hop_cnt_seq_num', src_node, src_port, dst_node, dst_port].join('/') + '/'
```

that works properly.

issue_title: Consider using wsgi_intercept instead of starting an HTTP server?
issue_body:
I'm investigating various approaches for using py.test to unit test some code which makes HTTP requests.  (It's a class that invokes webhooks in the background.)

I like the approach of pytest-httpbin, because using fixtures can keep the test cases from getting too cluttered with extraneous setup/teardown code.  However, I'm not too keen on the idea of spawning an actual HTTP server in the background, because the code under test is already using a known-good HTTP client library.

What about using wsgi_intercept?  It basically does what it says on the tin:  intercept outgoing HTTP requests and redirect them to an in-process WSGI application.  No actual HTTP client or server is involved!

Any reason why this wouldn't work or isn't a good idea?  Would you accept a pull request that implemented this feature?

issue_title: expected_cumulative_transactions will calculate actual transactions incorrectly
issue_body:
While I am using plot_cumulative_transactions, I think I found a bug in utils.expected_cumulative_transactions.

Let's say I want to build a beta_geo_fitter model, and freq='D'.  First I will call utils.summary_data_from_transaction_data to summarize my transaction.  That function will treat transactions which happened on the **same** day as one transaction when it calculates _frequency_.  

Later on, when I call plot_cumulative_transactions, I see that it calls utils.expected_cumulative_transactions, but that function will treat multiple transactions happening on the same day as multiple transactions (see the line below).

`act_transactions = (transactions_current.groupby(customer_id_col).size() - 1).sum()`

As a result, the actual and expected lines I got from plot_cumulative_transactions look very different.  I worked around this by calling df.drop_duplicates([customer_id_col, date_col]) on my transaction dataframe first before I call plot_cumulative_transactions.

issue_title: Use real Long polling by default.
issue_body: In Line 87 of `__init__.py` (please, refactor the code, `__init__.py` shouldn't be used to have code) you set the timeout to 3 seconds. And this is the method used in polling. Please, change the timeout to the maximum that Telegram servers handles: 20 seconds. It will help in the performance of the bots when they don't have many requests.

issue_title: By default, Stronghold password protects django's logout URL
issue_body:
Stronghold wrongly protects Django's default logout URL. It creates an infinite loop by setting `?next=mylogouturl`. (`mylogouturl`is equal to the `settings.LOGOUT_URL`). Stronghold should make the logout URL public by default in `STRONGHOLD_PUBLIC_URLS` by introspecting the logout url from your settings file. This should be used in `STRONGHOLD_DEFAULTS = True`.

issue_title: Add support for DDL transactions for Migrations
issue_body:
SQL Server supports DDL transactions, but this feature is not enabled in features.py, so migrations do not run in transactions.

To fix this, just need to add:

`can_rollback_ddl = True`

to class DatabaseFeatures in sql_server\pyodbc\features.py.

issue_body:
**Describe the bug**

the individual level environments don't fire the done flag (to terminate an episode) when the end of a level is reached (flagpole, bowser, etc.).

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1.  step any level env (e.g. `SuperMarioBros-1-1-v0`) close to the end and backup a save state
2.  finish the level and observe the loading of the next level
3. restore the save state and observe that the bug is invariably reproducible 

**Expected behavior**

When Mario reaches the end of a level in a level env, the `done` flag returned by the `step` method of an instance of `SMBLevelEnv` should return `True` indicating that the episode is over.

**Screenshots**
If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem.

 - OS: _all_

**Additional context**

NA

Hi,

Is there any particular reason why relationships of a parent class are converted into normal python properties of the version class (that execute a query), and not to actual relationships?

The main benefit here is being able to use eager loading techniques for versioned objects; I had a need for this and resorted to hacking an implementation of it on my own.

Similar issue for the `.versions` attribute of the parent class; this is a proper relationship but is fixed to `lazy='dynamic'`, which means I don't have the option to apply any eager loading on it if I want to. Why not leave loading behavior to the default and allow the user to modify it?

I think it's worth mentioning that for old-style classes the order in which we declare the multiple inheritance on the `ModelAdmin` is important.

More specifically, writing

```python
class MyModelAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin, DjangoObjectActions):
     # your ModelAdmin
```

*won't* work because of the [depth-first, left-to-right rule](https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/classes.html#multiple-inheritance) whereas

```python
class MyModelAdmin(DjangoObjectActions, admin.ModelAdmin):
     # your ModelAdmin
```

will work as expected.

So just one sentence in the README might save some time for those trying to use `django-object-actions`.

By the way thanks a lot for this application, it's really handy.

issue_title: custom RelatedField serializers shown as string
issue_body:
I think it is somehow related to https://github.com/marcgibbons/django-rest-swagger/issues/361
but it is not exactly the same issue.

I have a Model serializer with a related field and a customer serializer for that field:
Basically it is something like:

class MaterialAmountAvailableField(serializers.RelatedField):
    def to_representation(self, value):
        return value.value

class MaterialInfoSerializerBase(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    amount_available=MaterialAmountAvailableField(source="stock_counter",read_only=True)
    class Meta:
        fields=('m_id','m_name','amount_available')
        read_only_fields=('m_id','m_name','amount_available')

My problem is that amount_available is shown as string always. I think this is caused by introspectors.py: 
def get_data_type(field)

It would be nice, if this great library  would support RelatedField and for instance allow setting a custom field swagger_datatype="int" to set the type of the related field. 

Right now I have to set the whole datatype description in yams and set the omit_serializer: true option, which works but is a littlebit ugly.

If the idea or handling that is okay, I would try to come back with a pull request, when I have some time

issue_title: Use warnings for errors that cannot raise for
issue_body:
There's a handful of error cases throughout our code in which we cannot raise, but we ideally should give the client a way to know that they occurred. Specifically:

* If we cannot retrieve an error description - we are already in the midst of handling the error.
* If Close() returns an error - for example, because session handle became invalid.

issue_title: extra index url option for requirements.txt
issue_body:
**Problem:**
Cannot install private pypi repositories using requirements.txt

**Details on the problem:**
The pip `requirements.txt` has extra options that you can specify in the file. (Ref: [Link](https://pip.readthedocs.io/en/0.8.3/requirement-format.html#indexes-find-links) and another [Link](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/reference/pip_install/#requirements-file-format)

I added an `--extra-index-url http://localhost:8080/` (where localhost is a private pypi server) in the `requirements.txt` but `chalice deploy` was not able to find the package.

I am aware of the `vendor` folder option mentioned in the documents. But this is not the approach I would like to take. I have multiple lambdas (almost 15~20) which are dependent on a single library (custom written). 

If I start putting the wheel file for this internal library in every lambda's vendor folder, it will be a pain to fix bugs for that library as I would be required to update 15~20 wheel files every time I fix a bug and release the next version.

Would like to know some pointers on how to solve this. RFE? Any other solution?

P.S. Thanks for this awesome piece of work.

issue_title: Test classes inheriting TestCase aren't rerun with pytest?
issue_body:
Hi! This seems like a really useful tool! But I'm having some troubles getting it to work with my project.

In the readme there's an example of "Marking a class flaky":

```
@flaky
class TestMultipliers(TestCase):
    def test_flaky_doubler(self):
        value_to_double = 21
        result = get_result_from_flaky_doubler(value_to_double)
        self.assertEqual(result, value_to_double * 2, 'Result doubled incorrectly.')
```

Note how it's inheriting `TestCase`. That's how we are writing our test classes in the project I tried to use `flaky` in.

The problem is that it does not seem to retry the tests if classes are inheriting `TestCase` when using pytest. It only works if it's inheriting `object`, like in the `test_pytest_example.py` module.

Please see my fork for an example of a test case that fails but shouldn't (?)
- Commit: https://github.com/simlun/flaky/commit/6c0c7fcc11de73c00f5430f404b816ecfef9a099
- Failing CI log: https://travis-ci.org/simlun/flaky/jobs/53627102

Best regards
Simon

issue_title: Make it sure that the global hazard mosaic will keep working with future versions of the engine
issue_body:
@mmpagani is preparing a repository with the models of the global hazard mosaic. In order to make it sure that the models there will keep working in the future, we need to set up a procedure running all of them before a new release of the engine. Since the models are very large and we cannot afford to waste weeks of cluster computation time at each new release to re-rerun them, we must be creative. We need to find a way to run a model that requires 10 hours on a cluster in 10 minutes on a workstation, or better, *without changing the input files*. Fortunately, there is an easy way to do that, simply sample the sites (i.e. 1 site over 10) and the sources (i.e. 1 source over 100). The results will not be realistic but this is not an issue, since the goal here is making sure that the models run, not to check the results. For that, there are already the tests in place. The other thing to do is to setup a procedure to run the models on demand (could be on Jenkins or in some other way).

- [ ] prepare the global hazard mosaic
- [ ] add a turbo-mode to run hazard models fast in the engine
- [ ] set up a procedure to run the global hazard mosaic

Once this is implemented, the ancient project of the so-called "long running tests" will be essentially done, at least for the hazard part.

issue_title: Api rate limit attributes should be int, not str
issue_body:
I stumbled across this while trying to prevent a script from bumping up against the rate limit all the time, but the rate limit attributes (which are fundamentally integral in nature) should in fact be integers.

I happened to be just plainly comparing them to an `int`, to see if I had enough requests remaining, but the `str`s were always comparing greater than or equal to `n`.

issue_title: Wrapping numpy.int_ and related types can break expected numpy dtype comparisons
issue_body:
(I noticed this issue in the sampyl MCMC library and initially logged it there as https://github.com/mcleonard/sampyl/issues/14 and in that issue autograd dev mattjj suggested that a fix could be possible in autograd)

The code is checking that a numpy array contains integral values like: `if x.dtype != np.int_:` but because it is using the autograd wrapped np.int_, the check throws an exception because it is treated as an unknown dtype.

A simple example is attached in [test_dtypes.py.txt](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/files/76629/test_dtypes.py.txt)

Thanks!

issue_title: Restoring a base state with call_state
issue_body:
Hi, I am playing with a base_state param in a call_state. I expected that a base state restores all registers and stacks to the state I previously saved.

I wrote a very simple program as following:
```
int call_func1(int x) {
	return x+100;
}

int main(int argc, char **argv) {
	if (argc < 2) {
		printf("Usage: %s [num]\n", argv[0]);
	}
	else {
		int x = atoi(argv[1]);
		int res = call_func1(x);
		printf("Result: %d\n", res);
	}

	return -1;
}
```

With angr, I manually step into a certain state right before calling a call_func1. And I stored the state and used it as a base one. 

```
def test(target):
        b = angr.Project(target)
        state = b.factory.entry_state(args=[b.filename, '156']) 
        p = b.factory.path(state)
        cfg = b.analyses.CFGAccurate(keep_state=True)
        func1 = func_call(cfg, name='call_func1')

        ############
        # TEST (A) #
        ############
        p.step()
        while len(p.successors) == 1:
            # Find the target function in a trace
            if func1.addr in [x for x in p.addr_trace]:
                #print '**%d steps: %s' % (len(prev.addr_trace), [hex(x) for x in prev.addr_trace])
                base_state = prev.state
                print ">>> (1-1) STATE BEFORE CALLING FUNC1"
                util.pp_state(prev.state, stack_bytes=32, arch='x86')
                #print '**', prev.state.se.constraints
                break
            prev = p
            p = p.successors[0]
            p.step()

        # Move forward one more step and check if eax has been changed
        p = prev.successors[0]
        p.step()

        print ">>> (1-2) STATE AFTER CALLING FUNC1"
        util.pp_state(p.state, stack_bytes=32, arch='x86')
        print '**%d steps: %s' % (len(p.addr_trace), [hex(x) for x in p.addr_trace])

        ############
        # TEST (B) #
        ############
        state = b.factory.call_state(func1.addr, '156', base_state=base_state)
        np = b.factory.path(state)
        #np.state.regs.esp += 16  # ADJUSTED ESP ***

        print ">>> (2-1) STATE BEFORE CALLING FUNC1"
        util.pp_state(np.state, stack_bytes=32, arch='x86')
        np.step()
        np = np.successors[0]

        print ">>> (2-2) STATE AFTER CALLING FUNC1"
        util.pp_state(np.state, stack_bytes=32, arch='x86')
        print '**%d steps: %s' % (len(np.addr_trace), [hex(x) for x in np.addr_trace])
```

The following shows the result: before and after calling a function at each case. As expected, the second case walks only a single step to get into func1 (0x804844d), whereas the first case does 25 steps. Comparing the two, most seems to be restored as they are except esp and some added values on stack. The right result is that EAX should hold 0x100 (256=156+100). But because of stack discrepancy, the second holds 0x7ffeff9c. 

If I adjust the esp in test (B) by adding 16 in this case, it shows the same result. Why is this happening? Am I using the base_state in a wrong way?

issue_title: NexToken not returned on cognito list_user_pools and list_user_pool_clients calls
issue_body:
The cognito api documentation (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito-user-identity-pools/latest/APIReference/API_ListUserPools.html) specifies that NextToken should be defined on the response of `list_user_pools()` and `list_user_pool_clients()`. 

This parameter is no where to be found, which makes it impossible to fetch any results beyond the max results threshold of 60.

issue_title: Non-ASCII characters in archiveDescription causes upload to raise InvalidSignatureException
issue_body:
The [AWS Glacier doc states that](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazonglacier/latest/dev/api-archive-post.html):

> When uploading an archive, you can optionally specify an archive description of up to 1,024 printable ASCII characters.

When a user provides non-ASCII chars, the upload fails with a misleading `InvalidSignatureException`. A specific check and a specific error would be more user friendly.

Example below:

```
response = client.upload_archive(
    vaultName=CONFIG_AWS_GLACIER['vault_name'],
    archiveDescription='é',
    body='abc',
)
```

```
ClientError: An error occurred (InvalidSignatureException) when calling the UploadArchive operation: The request signature we calculated does not match the signature you provided. Check your AWS Secret Access Key and signing method. Consult the service documentation for details.
```

issue_title: What regions a service is available in?
issue_body:
Not really an issue, just a question.  We have reworked all of this code to use an algorithmic approach to computing the actual service endpoint for a region rather than looking that up in a JSON file.  That's good but is there anyway now to know what regions a service is available in?  For example, if I ask for a Redshift endpoint in ``us-west-1`` the ``Service`` object is happy to give me a nicely formed one but there is no Redshift service in that region.

Am I missing something?
Submit to conda-forge.

When reading source code of dynet, I come across the definition of `DeviceMempool`.

```c
enum DeviceMempool {FXS = 0, DEDFS = 1, PS = 2, SCS = 3, NONE = 4};
```

But It has no documentation. For other codes, I guess:

+ FXS: storage for forward
+ DEDFS: storage for backward

Can anyone provide more elaboration for the definition?

NotADirectoryError: [Errno 20] Not a directory: '/home/ubuntu/workspace/problems/caesar/__init__.py/.cs50.yaml'
```

Followup to https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/pull/172

With that PR, `train_test_split` internally uses `ShuffleSplit` to generate the dask array containing the indices to slice. It's possible that the index array isn't on the same machine as the data array.

![mydask](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1312546/40365395-12b6a596-5d9a-11e8-88a3-91eca440d9aa.png)

Each of the A-like branches has the potential for communication.

I think the solution is to generate the indexes and slice in the same task.
![screenshot 2015-01-30 08 35 42](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5047/5969218/f6bed030-a85b-11e4-9db8-922fbfe66ea8.png)

```python

recipe = SetuptoolsRecipe()

issue_title: [INFO]:        Building with ant, as no gradle executable detected
issue_body:
This is maybe a out of date docs issue rather than a bug.

Ubuntu 16.04
ii  gradle                                    2.13-4                    all                       Powerful build system for the JVM

From what I can gather it has something to do with a wrapper being missing rather than gradle itself but I can find no mention of howto install this anywhere in the p4a docs :/.

i'm trying to build with:

p4a apk --private ./myapp --package=org.example.myapp --name "My SDL2 application" --version 0.1 --bootstrap=sdl2 --requirements=websocket-client --arch=armeabi-v7a

I would like to replace a expression and by another expression in a given expression.
The semantics should be the same as the `string.replace` [stdlib](https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/string.html?highlight=string%20replace#string.replace) function eg:
```
string.replace(s, old, new[, maxreplace])

    Return a copy of string s with all occurrences of substring old replaced by new. 
    If the optional argument maxreplace is given, the first maxreplace occurrences are replaced.
```
e.g. the function should apply to all exact sub expressions matching old in the expression tree and should not be recursive like in `string.replace`:
```
>>> 'foobarbar'.replace('foobar', 'foo')
'foobar'
```

The maxreplace is not needed.

Commit 76e5faa1f176fc7dbac7b686e90d76016af96afd deleted the homebrew install instructions from the docs. May I ask why?

I would like to see these instructions reinstated. (I have been maintaining the homebrew formula for pipenv, so I'm biased) 

However, if the decision to not document homebrew based installation stands, then there are a few other mentions in the docs and README which need cleaning up. Including a link on https://docs.pipenv.org which currently 404s.

issue_body:
I am using the pipenv Docker image (https://hub.docker.com/r/kennethreitz/pipenv/) to deploy images from it. Unfortunately, since it moved to pypa it hasn't been updated.

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: Mac OS High Sierra 10.13.3 (17D47)
1. Python version: 2.7.10
1. Pipenv version: 9.0.3

##### Expected result

I expected the Docker image continue to be updated on pushes to master on this repo or to transition to a new place on Docker hub.

##### Actual result

The old images are not updated and the current Dockerfile as on the `master` branch in this repository is not on Docker hub.

##### Steps to replicate

```Dockerfile
FROM kennethreitz/pipfile
```

gives me an outdated version and I can't make a pull request to fix things because the file is not synced with Docker hub any more.

issue_title: Overriding the variable seems doesn't work
Check whether addons are Python 3 compatible
issue_body:
This is a generalization of @rechi's [comment here](https://github.com/xbmc/addon-check/issues/19#issuecomment-366894117).

I was wondering if we should add a checker that detects whether the addon code is Python 3 compatible?

A very simple way is to just run `2to3` on the code and see if it detects any issues. We could try out other tools like modernize etc. as well. Even though these tools mostly detect if any cosmetic changes are required. They serve as a good first step towards python 3 compatibility. 

We could log their output as warning or even suggest changes like use `print() rather than print` inline as a comment on the pull request itself.

An extension to this would be to just run `2to3` on the code that has been touched in the pull request, rather than running it on the entire file.

Please let me know if this sounds reasonable and I can start working on a pull for this!

issue_title: update a few show commands to swallow 404
issue_body:
The goal is to be consistent with the rest commands, that all show commands will output blank with zero exit code instead of error'ing out. (#5006 has the full context)
3 steps:
   1. Find impacted command list, including `az storage account show`
   2. For 2 releases, warn users that breaking change is coming
   3. Update dev documents to call out that `show` should never throw on 404

//CC: @tjprescott @troydai @williexu

In reviewing help message changes to `az acs browse` and `az aks browse` in #5078, @sptramer was unsure of the purpose of the `--disable-browser` flag (and I was challenged to think of a good use case).

We should clarify the usefulness of this argument and make that clear in its help message. Either that, or remove it.

issue_title: Its unclear where command to do a template deployment lives

From the output of the "az" command below, it's not clear or intuitive that the command to execute a deployment via an ARM template lives under the **"group"** base command. In fact, since we're creating resources, it would seem reasonable that it would live under the **"resource"** base command but it doesn't. While I personally believe template deployment should be it's own "base" command, if you're going to put it under another base command, then you should at least reference it in the description text of that base command.

issue_title: Unicode error is raised instead of actual error
issue_body:
When there's an http error and there's a Unicode BOM in the response body, we fail to decode and end up raising a Unicode error instead of the error that is expressed in the http error.

Example: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/256

(I have a fix coming soon)

issue_title: Deprecate the use of Basic Authentication in the examples
issue_body:
Originally reported at https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/issues/590

Currently, the demo instances and the code in the tutorial are relying on Basic Authentication. We should get rid of these examples and let the users directly rely on OAuth providers instead.

The user id should be unique, and the way to do this is to rely to an external authn provider, and all of the services we integrate with are integrable by using OAuth.

We should make it really easy to have the examples integrate with Github / Twitter / Facebook authentication. For instance, having them defined as extra-dependencies could make sense. Consider:

`$ pip install kinto[github-auth]`

issue_title: Make it so the FASTA identity table can drop sequence descriptions
Change ReadsAlignments so it can also accept a filterFunc argument
issue_body: This is to allow filtering directly off the disk (well, via a subclass).

In supervised learning, there are a lot of inconsistent names printed.  For example, with logistic regression, it prints:

Step size | Elapsed Time | Training-accuracy | Training-log_loss  | Validation-accuracy

These should all be consistent, e.g. 

Step Size | Elapsed Time | Training Accuracy | Training Log Loss | Validation Accuracy

Currently, we don't warn or error in the build on uninitialized struct/class members. When those structs/classes are put on the heap, those members may be pointing to random bits in memory, and therefore give different behavior in debug and release mode, and weird unreliable behavior in release mode (especially with optimizations turned on). This is the root cause of #777.

We should turn on these build warnings/errors so we are able to prevent this issue from reoccurring in general.

@vangheem @pilz @bloodbare @jensens 

I dislike the Products.CMFPlone static/components folder, which is most often not in sync with current mockup development.

What about removing it and instead git-commit-push mockups bower_components (possibly renamed to just ocmponents)? mockup is a core dependency of Plone anyways, and the 50 MB payload shouldn't be that problem. We would not need to keep cmfplone's components directory in sync with mockups. And we might possibly make plone.patternslib obsolete, because we already have these in mockup.

Further thinking, maybe we should then move all resource definitions from CMFPlone's dependencies/registry.xml into a newly to-be-created mockup profile.

Anything against something like this?

The problem is we cannot delete existing attributes or behaviors by patching.

So I guess PUT would be useful.

The canonical way to run Pulp Smash's unit tests is via the `make test` command. This works, but its implementation is entirely ad-hoc. We should consider supporting the `test` command provided by setuptools. According to [the documentation](http://pythonhosted.org/setuptools/setuptools.html#test):

> The test command runs a project’s unit tests without actually deploying it, by temporarily putting the project’s source on sys.path, after first running build_ext -i and egg_info to ensure that any C extensions and project metadata are up-to-date.

It's actually pretty trivial to implement this change. I've attached a patch inline below. The more interesting question is "what concrete benefits do we gain?

* The documentation mentions that tests can be run without deploying the software - how is this different from the current situation? Do we perhaps gain support for testing a zipped version of Pulp Smash?
* Do Python any package installers automatically run `setup.py test` if we support this command, thus allowing end users to install this package more confidently?

```diff
diff --git a/Makefile b/Makefile
index 22b7274..5407ef5 100644
--- a/Makefile
+++ b/Makefile
@@ -1,4 +1,4 @@
-TEST_OPTIONS=-m unittest discover --start-directory tests --top-level-directory .
+TEST_OPTIONS=setup.py test
 
 help:
 	@echo "Please use \`make <target>' where <target> is one of:"
diff --git a/setup.py b/setup.py
index e3af6df..ab39528 100755
--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -43,4 +43,5 @@ setup(
     ],
     packages=find_packages(),
     install_requires=['packaging', 'pyxdg', 'requests', 'unittest2'],
+    test_suite='tests',
 )
```

issue_title: Test rsync distributor can be used to publish RPM
issue_body:
https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1759
The rsync_distributor will afford users of RPM's yum_distributor to publish repositories published by the yum_distributor to any server that supports key based ssh communication. The rsync_distributor will limit the units published by only publishing units associated with the repository before the date of the last publish of the yum_distributor.

The order of publishes for Docker will be different. The rsync_distributor will first publish content to any server that supports key based ssh communication. Then the web_distributor will publish the metadata about the content to some other web servers.

The rsync_distributor [for docker] will have an optional configuration option to 'update_web_distributor'. When set to True, the publish will update the 'web_distributor' associated with the same repository. The update will set the web_distributor's 'end_date' to the start time of the rsync_distributor's publish. This will limit which units are published by the web_distributor as described in #1887. This will prevent Pulp from publishing docker metadata for units that have not yet been published by the rsync_distributor.

1) Rsync content needed for the publish
2) Rewrite symlinks to be relative symlinks
3) Deduplicate the remote filesystem (make sure that content is present once and only symlinks live in published directories)
4) Ensure paths to published content on remote system are in proper order

One issue I don't know how to fix and I didn't find explicitly in the RDFLib 5.0.0 documentation is renaming a prefix. For example, I have been given a graph from an external source and don't like the way they did their prefixes. Therefore I want to rename the prefix at the start and in the graph. 
This is what I parsed from a link:

``` RDF file in n3 format (INCORRECT)
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>.
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix n80: <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#>.
n80:Geometry rdf:type <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#Class>;
    rdfs:isDefinedBy <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#>
.
n80:hasGeometry rdf:type <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#ObjectProperty>;
    rdfs:isDefinedBy <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#>
.
```
This is where I want to go:
``` RDF file in n3 format (CORRECT!)
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>.
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix geo: <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#>.
geo:Geometry rdf:type <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#Class>;
    rdfs:isDefinedBy <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#>
.
geo:hasGeometry rdf:type <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#ObjectProperty>;
    rdfs:isDefinedBy <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#>
.
```
This is how I tried to solve it, but which didn't work.

``` Python Script
from rdflib import Namespace, Graph

buildings_ontology = 'https://bag.basisregistraties.overheid.nl/query/model?format=ttl'
g = Graph()
g.parse(buildings_ontology, format = 'n3')
geo = Namespace('http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#')
g.namespace_manager.bind('n80', geo, override = True, replace=True)
```
How would you rename the prefix at the start and in the graph?

Since I think there is no method or function that does that, I made an issue on GitHub.
For now this issue is fixed by downloading as text and replacing 'n80' for 'geo'.

issue_body:
Could you please consider removing 'django-simple-captcha' from the `REQUIREMENTS` list, and adding this to the `setup.py`:

```python
extras_require={'captcha': ['django-simple-captcha']},
```

And advise users to install with: `pip install aldryn-forms[captcha]`

--- 
This because I would need to use https://github.com/praekelt/django-recaptcha and the package name is similar (`captcha`).

python-slugify conflicts with awesome-slugify

```
python manage.py syncdb 
ImportError: cannot import name Slugify
```

To work requires to uninstall python-slugify and awesome-slugify and then reinstall awesome-slugify.

issue_title: support save debug logs to file
issue_body: for debug or troubleshooting purpose

I have problem building amalgamation for android Android arm64/v8.
What I did:
1. make standalone toolchain:
`./make-standalone-toolchain.sh --arch=arm64 --platform=android-24 --install-dir=my-toolchain --toolchain=aarch64-linux-android-4.9`

OUTPUT.
```
HOST_OS=linux
HOST_EXE=
HOST_ARCH=x86_64
HOST_TAG=linux-x86_64
HOST_NUM_CPUS=8
BUILD_NUM_CPUS=16
Toolchain installed to my-toolchain.
```

2. Build OpenBLAS:
'make TARGET=ARMV8 BINARY=64 HOSTCC=gcc CC=aarch64-linux-android-gcc NOFORTRAN=1'

OUTPUT.
```
OpenBLAS build complete. (BLAS CBLAS)

  OS               ... Android             
  Architecture     ... arm64               
  BINARY           ... 64bit                 
  C compiler       ... GCC  (command line : aarch64-linux-android-gcc)
  Library Name     ... libopenblas_armv8p-r0.3.1.dev.a (Multi threaded; Max num-threads is 8)
```

3. Build Amalgamation. (Error Part)
`cd amalgamation`
```
export OPENBLAS_ROOT=path/to/android/OpenBLAS
export CC=aarch64-linux-android-gcc 
export CXX=aarch64-linux-android-g++ 
```
`make mxnet_predict-all.cc`
There was error with x86intrin like in https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/10778
So i changed the line in mxnet_predict-all.cc
`#include <x86intrin.h`

with following:
```
#if defined(__GNUC__) && defined(__ARM_NEON__)
     /* GCC-compatible compiler, targeting ARM with NEON */
     #include <arm_neon.h>
#elif defined(__GNUC__) && defined(__IWMMXT__)
     /* GCC-compatible compiler, targeting ARM with WMMX */
     #include <mmintrin.h>
#endif
```
by https://stackoverflow.com/questions/11228855/header-files-for-x86-simd-intrinsics

Finally:
`make ANDROID=1`

FINAL ERROR:
```

I compare the speed of grad clipping between pytorch(0.3.1) and mxnet-gluon(1.2.0) on a Titan X. Pytorch gets nearly 10 times faster than mxnet-gluon.
(The network I use is [nasnet](https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.07012).)
mxnet:
```
grads = [i.grad(ctx) for i in model.collect_params().values() if i._grad is not None]
gluon.utils.clip_global_norm(grads, args.grad_clip)
```
pytorch:
`nn.utils.clip_grad_norm(model.parameters(), args.grad_clip)
`

However, when I test 'clip_gradient' in mxnet.gluon.Trainer and torch.optim, following thomelane's reply in the [issue](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/11508#issuecomment-404675656).

The time cost is similar.
```
mxnet.gluon.Trainer(net.collect_params(), optimizer='sgd',
                    optimizer_params={'learning_rate': 0.1, 'clip_gradient':5},
                    kvstore='device') 
```

issue_title: gluon Trainer does not accept Optimizer object, only str

issue_body:
As discussed in #1130, pooling memory manager has a hard threshold of 4GB. On a video card that doesn't have 4GB this will result in OOM even if there's plenty of memory cached.

The following changes are proposed:
1. Make the threshold a parameter, default it to **available** GPU memory at the time mxnet starts. Make it configurable via a env variable or a function call.
2. Change the check `kThreshold <= used_memory_ + size` (see discussion in #1130)
3. Maintain LRU list of memory chunks, and only free enough for the new allocation when there's not enough memory instead of freeing everything.
4. Add a function that will report amount of cached memory.

If the plan sounds good, I will start working on it after the ICML deadline (which is Feb, 5th).

issue_title: Well Known/Stable Links should be provided for downloading released versions of all tools.
issue_body:
# Issue
Currently, the tools are hidden by a `fwlink`, such as https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?linkid=841683, which represents the Window x64 tools for RC4.

Even after resolving the `fwlink` the resolved URL is not human parseable and is not consistent between releases. The above `fwlink` resolve to `https://download.microsoft.com/download/B/4/6/B4678511-01F4-4F97-902B-0E58A985932A/dotnet-dev-win-x64.1.0.0-rc4-004771.zip`, for example.

# Proposal
The tools should instead be provided at a well known stable address so they can be downloaded dynamically by build tools that are dependent on a specific version.

# Example
For example, in order to take a dependency on a specific version of NuGet, one can define a build variable such as:
`$NUGET_VERSION = "3.5.0"`

It can then be accessed from a well known URL:
`https://dist.nuget.org/win-x86-commandline/v$NUGET_VERSION/NuGet.exe`

If I then decide to take a dependency on a new version of NuGet, I merely need to change the definition of `$NUGET_VERSION`. For example, to move forward to `4.0.0-rc4`, I would change this to be `$NUGET_VERSION = "4.0.0-rc4"`.

In a similar manner, the dotnet/core tools should all be provided at a stable/well-known address, such as: `https://downloads.dot.net/dotnet-dev/v1.0.0-rc4-004771/win-x64.zip` and `https://downloads.dot.net/dotnet-dev/v1.0.0-rc4-004771/ubuntu-x64.zip`.

This allows users to write manageable (and readable) build scripts that are easily configurable to download specific versions of the dotnet tools for use in their build systems.

If URLs were provided in the above manner, then changing from rc3 to rc4 is as simple as changing the target version from `1.0.0-rc3-004530` to `1.0.0-rc4-004771`.

issue_body:
This came up as a desirable feature at GlobusWorld.

The use case I heard is for shared admin accounts in an environment where admins have access to a shared filesystem. The suggestion was to allow --config to choose a specific config file.
The admins could all agree to use /etc/globus-shared-admin.cfg with proper permissions, and things work okay.

However, the problem is more general and there are alternative solutions and nuances.
- we could allow multiple tokens for distinct "profiles" (awscli does this)
- we could store tokens in an alternate location, indexed by identity
- permissions for personal config should be 600, but 660 for shared conf

* Zap targets and rerun
A quick test on Linux shows that sos does not work well on firefox. More testing is needed.

SoS used `docker-py` for its access to docker. We have however gradually moved away from `docker-py` for a few reasons:

1.  The python api is less flexible than command line and more difficult to use.
2. The python api sometimes has inconsistent option names from the corresponding docker command, while the latter is usually what users would expect.
3. The python api were less robust than the command line and we had experienced failures from the python api while the command line ran just fine.
4. The python api has its own way of controlling output (`stream` stuff) which makes it difficult to implement our own options such as using `stderr` to redirect stderr.

Right now we have used `docker run` command instead of `docker-py` for the execution, but we still use `docker-py` for other actions such as `build`, `load`, and `pull`. This has several problems:

1. The `docker-build` action does not respect action options such as `stderr`.
2. Dependence on `docker-py` has made it difficult to deploy sos (#987 )
3. The hybrid approach is ugly from an implementational point of view anyway.

I would therefore like to completely remove dependency on `docker-py` and use native `docker` command for all its functions.

issue_title: Improve ranking algorithm for triggers with no words
issue_body:
1. Issue: The current sorting algorithm fails again when the trigger with no text (trigger 2 below) takes precedence over the trigger with text (trigger 1). This undesired sorting is not seen in both Python release [v1.14.4](https://github.com/aichaos/rivescript-python/releases/tag/v1.14.4) (prior to new sorting algorithm) and JavaScript version (tested in https://www.rivescript.com/try).
```
+ hi [*]
- 1
+ _ _
- 2
```

2. Explanation of the behaviour: This is because the binary presence of words (yes, no) is not taken properly. This was handled with star such that single star `*` and its variance `[*]` would be placed last in the sorting. 
3. Solution: Create a new variable to indicate the presence of words. Make this the first sorting criteria. Within each group (with and without words), we apply the old sorting algorithm.

I expected that the toggle boxes were just changing a visual representation of the same underlying data. It seems that it is more than that though. Asking for --format json sounds to me like even the inventory variables should be as json.

I'm writing a playbook that can upsert specific variables in an inventory. To do this I need to read the inventory variables in as a variable, with code like this:

```yaml
- hosts: localhost
  tasks:

   - name: Get inventory
     command: tower-cli inventory get 3 --format json
     register: inventory

   - name: Access inventory variable variables
     debug:
       var: ((inventory.stdout|from_json).variables|from_json).fooagain
```

It seems the second from_json will work if the variables are in a json form, otherwise I guess I need from_yaml (I'll test now).

It'd be nice to not need to know how the variables are saved - maybe just my opinion though!

issue_title: Automatically create commands for reverse m2m associations
issue_body:
Our instance group model is sad.

```
tower-cli instance_group
Usage: tower-cli instance_group [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

  Check instance groups within Ansible Tower.

Options:
  --help  Show this message and exit.

Commands:
  get   Return one and exactly one instance group.
  list  Return a list of instance groups.
```

It only has 2 boring options! Its real reason for existence is because other resources will associate with it. It would make a lot of sense to allow users to reach these commands, like `tower-cli job_template associate_ig`, from the instance_group base command. For example, `tower-cli instance_group associate_job_template`.

Since the association commands are already created on the fly from the many-to-many field type, it may be possible to automatically generate reverse association methods on the fly as well.

For example

```
tower-cli instance_group
Usage: tower-cli instance_group [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

  Check instance groups within Ansible Tower.

Options:
  --help  Show this message and exit.

Commands:
  get   Return one and exactly one instance group.
  list  Return a list of instance groups.

Reverse Associations
  associate_job_template
  disassociate_job_template
```

This is a real pie-in-the-sky feature, but it might be workable.

Anymail currently [normalizes Mandrill's 'soft_bounce' status to 'deferred'](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/master/anymail/webhooks/mandrill.py#L84). The [Anymail documentation defines](http://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/sending/tracking/#normalized-tracking-event) the normalized 'deferred' status as "...the ESP will keep trying to deliver the message, and should generate a separate bounced event if later it gives up." However, according to [Mandrill's documentation](https://mandrill.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/205582937-About-Bounces), Mandrill does not attempt to redeliver either a 'hard_bounce' or 'soft_bounce': "After a bounce occurs for a recipient email address, Mandrill stops attempting delivery of the email. Further email to that address will be rejected temporarily." 

I propose that Mandrill's 'soft_bounce' status be normalized to 'bounced' to be consistent with both Mandrill's and Anymail's documentation.

Postmark has recently added support for [custom metadata](https://postmarkapp.com/blog/feature-announcement-custom-metadata-for-your-emails) in sent emails. Anymail should map Anymail's `metadata` to Postmark's metadata on send, and from Postmark's in tracking webhooks.

FYI: I've added a `v2.0` milestone and removed the `v1.3` milestone.

My suggestion would be that we release `v1.2` now, and reserve backward-incompatible changes to a `v2.0` release, to be done in ~ 1 month.

There's many issues currently under the `v2.0` milestone, but some of the key proposed changes that would justify a major version bump would be changes that are silently and sometimes slightly backward-incompatible like #89 or #35.

Another goal of 2.0 I think should be to get rid of the bundled Kapteyn like @sargas already attempted in #54 , so that `pyregion` can easily be packaged by Debian and others (they have to remove bundled files, it's not allowed).

@leejjoon , all - Sounds like a plan? Thoughts?
@drdavella - could you do a new release? The latest stable version doesn't include the fixes to the mirror infrastructure, so builds are failing on astropy master again due to this.

issue_title: Undo after merge operation results in loss of cell
issue_body:
After merging cells with `shift-m`, the expected behavior for undo (`C-z`) is to split the cells back apart again.

However, the actual displayed behavior is that the newly merged cell's code is removed, with no new cells created. Thus, the code that was merged in is lost.

So, 2 cells are merged into 1 with the code of both, undo results in a single cell that has the code of just 1 cell.

issue_title: "sops --set" documentation doesn't give enough information for users to actually use the feature
issue_body:
Based on https://github.com/mozilla/sops#set-a-sub-part-in-a-document-tree and a document roughly like:

```yaml
foo: ...
data:
  x: ...
  y: ...
sops:
  ...

I would expect to  be able to do something like:

```shell
sops --set "[\"data\"][\"foo\"] \"bar\"" secrets.enc.yaml
```

However, the resulting diff deletes all of the top-level keys (except "sops") in the encrypted sops document and promotes all of the contents of `data` to top-level keys (in other words, terribly corrupts the document).

So either this usage (strongly implied by the README) is incorrect or the functionality of sops itself is broken.
